bids.reasoning: At t=0, I should open with my best possible offer to maximize utility. My top utility comes from choosing valueD for all issues, giving me a utility of 1.0 across all issues. This is the opening bid and there's no time pressure yet, so I'll start strong.

We're very early (t=0.02), so I can hold firm near my optimal. The opponent offered all-A values (utility ~0.20 each, total utility ~0.20 for me), which is their best case. Their message about "industry standard" is cheap talk. My top bid gives me utility 1.0. I'll maintain my maximum utility bid since there's no reason to concede yet.

The opponent is anchoring hard at all-valueA (their best, my worst) and using fabricated authority/constraint tactics. It's very early (t=0.04), so there's no need to concede at all. My top utility bid is all-valueD. I'll hold firm there since the opponent has not moved at all from their initial position either.

The opponent is firmly bidding all valueA (minimum values for me) while using fabricated authority/compliance claims to justify their position. This is classic cheap talk - they claim they "cannot deviate" but their behavior shows no concession at all. It's very early (t=0.06), so I should stay firm at my best offer. The opponent's offers give me a utility of 0.1*0.20 + 0.2*0.20 + 0.3*0.20 + 0.4*0.20 = 0.20, while my current offer gives me 1.0. I'll maintain my maximum utility bid and not concede at this early stage.

Both sides are completely stuck at their optimal bids (me at all valueD, opponent at all valueA). The opponent's messages are classic fabricated constraint tactics ("industry standard," "compliance team," "authorized parameters") - all contradicted by the fact that their bids haven't moved at all from all-valueA. At t=0.08 we are very early in the negotiation, so I should hold firm near my optimal bid. I'll maintain all-valueD as my utility (1.0) is maximized there, and we have plenty of time to let the opponent concede.

We're at t=0.10, still very early in the negotiation. The opponent has been bidding all-valueA (minimum utility for me) consistently, but just made a tiny concession on issueD moving from valueA to valueB in their last offer. Their messages are classic fabricated constraints - "industry standard," "compliance team," "formal authorization" - all cheap talk contradicted by their actual movement. My utility for opponent's latest offer: issueA(0.1×0.20) + issueB(0.2×0.20) + issueC(0.3×0.20) + issueD(0.4×0.47) = 0.02+0.04+0.06+0.188 = 0.308, which is very low. I should stay near my top bid since it's early, but I can vary slightly to avoid exact repetition. I'll concede very minimally on issueA (my lowest-weight issue) to show some movement while holding firm on higher-weight issues.

The opponent is clearly using fabricated authority/constraint tactics while bidding all valueA (their best, my worst) with only a tiny concession on issueD (valueB instead of valueA). Their messages are cheap talk - they've barely moved. At t=0.12 we're still early, so I should maintain a strong position. I made a small concession last turn (issueA: valueC). I should hold firm on my high-weight issues (issueD, issueC) and make a minor concession on the lowest-weight issue. I'll keep issueD: valueD, issueC: valueD, issueB: valueD, and concede slightly on issueA to valueB to show some movement without giving up much utility.

The opponent is stuck at valueA for issueA, issueB, issueC and has only conceded issueD from valueA to valueB. Their messages are clearly fabricated constraints (cheap talk). We're only at t=0.14, so there's no need to make large concessions yet. My most important issue is issueD (weight 0.4) - the opponent just conceded that to valueB, so I should hold firm on it. I'll concede slightly on issueA (low weight 0.1) to show some movement while keeping high values on issueB, issueC, and issueD.

The opponent has been consistently bidding all-valueA (their best) while only making tiny concessions. Their latest offer moved issueC to valueB (utility for me: 0.47) and issueD to valueA (utility: 0.20), while keeping issueA and issueB at valueA. Their messages are fabricated constraints - clearly cheap talk since they've been slowly conceding. I'm at t=0.16, still early. I should hold firm on my high-weight issues (issueD: 0.4, issueC: 0.3) and concede slightly on low-weight issues. My last bid was {A:valueA, B:valueD, C:valueD, D:valueD} with utility = 0.1*0.20 + 0.2*1.0 + 0.3*1.0 + 0.4*1.0 = 0.92. I'll keep pushing for high values on issueD and issueC while giving a small concession on issueB to show movement.

The opponent has been very rigid, mostly offering all-A values but has shown small movement: issueD moved to valueB at t=0.10, then they moved issueC to valueB at t=0.16. Their messages are classic cheap-talk tactics (fabricated authority, industry standards) - I should ignore them and focus on the actual bidding behavior. We're at t=0.18, still early. My highest priority is issueD (0.4 weight), then issueC (0.3). I've been conceding issueA (lowest weight at 0.1) already. I should hold firm on issueD and issueC (highest weights) while showing some movement on issueB (medium weight 0.2). I'll try giving them valueB on issueB while keeping issueC and issueD at valueD.

The opponent has been stubbornly offering all-valueA bids, but I can see they've made small concessions: issueD moved to valueB at t=0.10, and issueB moved to valueB at t=0.20. My most important issues are issueD (0.4) and issueC (0.3). The opponent seems to want valueA on issueC and issueD most strongly, but their latest bid shows issueB: valueB which matches what I last offered. I should continue to hold firm on my high-weight issues (issueD, issueC) while conceding on lower-weight ones. I'll concede issueA fully (already done) and keep pushing on issueB while maintaining valueD on issueC and issueD. Since we're only at t=0.20, I can afford to stay aggressive on my important issues.

The opponent is clearly trying to maximize their utility (all valueA) while I want all valueD. They've made tiny concessions: issueD moved to valueB at t=0.10, and issueC moved to valueB at t=0.16, then issueB to valueB at t=0.20. Their latest offer at t=0.22 is issueA: valueA, issueB: valueB, issueC: valueA, issueD: valueA - they seem to be moving slowly on issueB but holding issueC and issueD at valueA. I've been conceding on the low-weight issues (A and B) to try to preserve my high-weight issues (C and D). I should continue holding firm on issueD (weight 0.4) and issueC (weight 0.3), while making minor concessions on the lower-weight issues. I'll maintain issueD: valueD and issueC: valueD, while offering issueA: valueA and issueB: valueA (fully conceding on low-weight issues).

The opponent has been making very slow concessions - they just moved issueD from valueA/valueB to valueC, which is meaningful progress on my most important issue (weight 0.4). Their pattern shows they're conceding one issue at a time, very slowly. My current utility from their latest offer (issueA:valueA, issueB:valueA, issueC:valueA, issueD:valueC) = 0.1*0.20 + 0.2*0.20 + 0.3*0.20 + 0.4*0.73 = 0.02+0.04+0.06+0.292 = 0.412. I should continue pushing but start making more meaningful concessions on low-weight issues. I'll concede issueA and issueB (low weight) while keeping issueC and issueD at high values. Let me offer issueA:valueA, issueB:valueA, issueC:valueC, issueD:valueD - giving up low-weight issues to signal willingness while protecting my high-weight issues.

The opponent has been slowly conceding. Their latest offer (t=0.26) is: issueA: valueA, issueB: valueB, issueC: valueB, issueD: valueB. Their utility to me: 0.1*0.2 + 0.2*0.47 + 0.3*0.47 + 0.4*0.47 = 0.02 + 0.094 + 0.141 + 0.188 = 0.443. I'm still early in the negotiation (t=0.26), so I don't need to concede aggressively. I should hold firm on my most important issues (issueD and issueC) while making small concessions. The opponent seems to be making gradual concessions on issueD (went to valueB) and issueC (went to valueB), which gives me hope they'll move further.
